metadata.title: Codestar: Machine Learning
metadata.subtitle: Interactive ML — from linear regression to neural networks
metadata.philosophy: Learn by exploring. Every concept has an interactive lab.

placementQuiz.title: Placement Q1
placementQuiz.prompt: What does supervised learning require that unsupervised learning does not?
placementQuiz.feedback: Supervised learning needs input-output pairs (labels). Unsupervised learns from unlabelled data.

placementQuiz.title: Placement Q2
placementQuiz.prompt: Which metric measures the average squared difference between predictions and actual values?
placementQuiz.feedback: MSE = mean((y_pred - y_true)^2). It penalises large errors heavily.

placementQuiz.title: Placement Q3
placementQuiz.prompt: A model scores 99% accuracy on training data but 55% on test data. What is happening?

choices.text: The learning rate is too high

placementQuiz.feedback: Very high train accuracy with low test accuracy is classic overfitting.

placementQuiz.title: Placement Q4
placementQuiz.prompt: In gradient descent, what does the gradient tell you?
placementQuiz.feedback: The gradient points uphill. We subtract it to move downhill toward the minimum.

placementQuiz.title: Placement Q5
placementQuiz.prompt: Which activation function outputs values strictly between 0 and 1?
placementQuiz.feedback: Sigmoid maps any real number to (0,1). Tanh maps to (-1,1). ReLU maps to [0,+inf).

questions.title: Concept
questions.prompt: Machine learning lets computers learn patterns from data instead of being explicitly programmed. A spam filter that improves as it sees more emails is ML; a hard-coded keyword list is not.
questions.feedback: ML = learning patterns from data, not explicit programming.

questions.title: Check Your Understanding
questions.prompt: Which of the following is machine learning?

choices.text: A rule flagging emails containing 'FREE MONEY' as spam
A model trained on millions of emails to predict spam probability
A for-loop that sorts a list alphabetically
A lookup table of known spam senders

questions.feedback: Learning from data (millions of emails) is the hallmark of ML.

questions.title: Fill the blank
questions.prompt: In supervised learning, the correct output for each training example is called a ___.
questions.feedback: Labels are the ground-truth outputs that supervise the model.

questions.title: Match the type
questions.prompt: Match each ML type to its description.

matchingPairs.right: Learns from labelled examples
Finds hidden patterns without labels
Learns by trial, error, and rewards

questions.feedback: These three paradigms cover the majority of real ML applications.

questions.title: Which type?
questions.prompt: A robot earns +1 when it stays upright and -1 when it falls. This is:
questions.feedback: Rewards and penalties that guide behaviour = reinforcement learning.

questions.title: Concept
questions.prompt: Features are the measurable inputs to a model (e.g. house size, bedrooms). Labels are the outputs we want to predict (e.g. house price). Choosing good features — feature engineering — often matters more than which algorithm you pick.
questions.feedback: Features in, labels out. Good features make everything easier.

questions.title: Identify the feature
questions.prompt: Building a diabetes prediction model. Which of these is a feature (input), not a label?
questions.feedback: Blood glucose is a measurable input (feature). Has diabetes = the label.

questions.title: sklearn fit call
questions.prompt: In sklearn, if X is your feature matrix and y your labels, the training call is model.fit(___, y).
questions.feedback: model.fit(X, y) is the standard sklearn training call.

questions.title: Why split?
questions.prompt: Why must you evaluate a model on data it has never seen during training?

choices.text: Test data trains the model faster

questions.feedback: Generalisation is the whole point. A model that only works on training data is useless.

questions.title: Code it
questions.prompt: Implement train_test_split_indices(n, test_frac=0.2) returning (train_size, test_size). test_size = round(n * test_frac).
questions.feedback: test_size = round(n * test_frac); train_size = n - test_size

questions.title: sklearn workflow
questions.prompt: Arrange these sklearn steps in the correct order.
questions.code:
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
questions.rawMarkdown:
Blocks:
```python
model.fit(X_train, y_train)
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y)
model = LinearRegression()
predictions = model.predict(X_test)
```
*Correct order:*
```python
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y)
model = LinearRegression()
model.fit(X_train, y_train)
predictions = model.predict(X_test)
```
questions.feedback: Split → Instantiate → Fit → Predict. The standard sklearn pipeline.

questions.title: Vocabulary match
questions.prompt: Match each term to its definition.

matchingPairs.right: Input variable used for prediction
Target output the model learns to predict
Performance on unseen data
Memorising training data, failing on test data

questions.feedback: Solid vocabulary is the foundation of every ML discussion.

questions.title: Lab: Linear Regression
questions.prompt: Drag the data points to see how the regression line and R² score change. Try arranging points in a line, then scatter them randomly. What happens to R²?
questions.feedback: The regression line minimises the sum of squared residuals. R² measures how well it fits.

questions.title: What does R² = 0 mean?
questions.prompt: When you scattered points randomly, R² dropped toward 0. What does R² = 0 mean?

choices.text: The model perfectly fits the data
The model is no better than predicting the mean

questions.feedback: R² = 1 is perfect; R² = 0 means the model explains nothing beyond the mean.

questions.title: Slope and intercept
questions.prompt:
A linear model is: y_hat = w * x + b

w (weight/slope) — how much y_hat changes per unit of x
b (bias/intercept) — value of y_hat when x = 0

Fitting the model means finding the w and b that minimise prediction error on training data.
questions.feedback: w and b are the two parameters we learn.

questions.title: Predict with the equation
questions.prompt: A model has w = 2.5, b = 10. Predict y_hat when x = 4. Answer: ___

questions.title: Mean Squared Error
questions.prompt: Implement mse(y_true, y_pred) that computes the mean squared error.
questions.feedback: sum((t - p)**2 for t, p in zip(y_true, y_pred)) / len(y_true)

questions.title: Parameter count
questions.prompt: You have 3 features (x1, x2, x3). How many parameters does a linear model have?
questions.feedback: y_hat = w1*x1 + w2*x2 + w3*x3 + b — 3 weights + 1 bias = 4 parameters.

questions.title: Dot product prediction
questions.prompt: Implement predict_linear(X, weights, bias). X is a list of feature vectors. Return a list of predictions using the dot product.
questions.feedback: sum(w * x for w, x in zip(weights, row)) + bias for each row in X

questions.title: Reading coefficients
questions.prompt:
In a house-price model: price = 150*sqft + 10000*bedrooms - 5000*age + 50000

• Each extra sqft adds $150
• Each bedroom adds $10,000  
• Each year of age reduces price by $5,000
• The intercept ($50,000) is the base price

Larger absolute coefficient = stronger influence (when features are on the same scale).
questions.feedback: Coefficients reveal which features matter most to the model.

questions.title: Which feature matters most?
questions.prompt: salary = 500*experience + 200*gpa_scaled + 100*projects + 30000. All features are on a 0-10 scale. Which has the strongest influence?
questions.feedback: When features are on the same scale, the largest absolute coefficient = strongest predictor.

questions.title: Compute R²
questions.prompt: Implement r_squared(y_true, y_pred). Formula: R² = 1 - SS_res / SS_tot, where SS_res = sum((y - y_hat)^2) and SS_tot = sum((y - y_mean)^2).
questions.feedback: SS_res = sum((t-p)**2); mean_y = sum(y_true)/len(y_true); SS_tot = sum((t-mean_y)**2)

questions.title: Quick recap
questions.prompt: Match each concept to its role in linear regression.

matchingPairs.right: Loss function to minimise
Measures fraction of variance explained
Intercept — output when all features = 0
Slope — how output changes per feature unit

questions.feedback: Strong foundations here will carry you all the way to neural networks.

questions.title: Lab: Gradient Descent
questions.prompt: Start the ball at x = 3.5. Press Step to take one gradient descent step. Observe the gradient arrow and position. Reset and try different learning rates.
questions.feedback: GD subtracts the gradient x learning rate from the current parameter at every step.

questions.title: The update rule
questions.prompt: In gradient descent: x ← x − α · ∇L. The symbol α (alpha) is the ___.
questions.feedback: The learning rate controls the step size at each iteration.

questions.title: Lab: Learning Rate Comparison
questions.prompt: Compare three learning rates: too small (slow convergence), just right (smooth descent), and too large (oscillates/diverges). How do their loss curves differ?
questions.feedback: A rate too large overshoots the minimum. Too small wastes compute. The sweet spot converges efficiently.

questions.title: What happens with lr = 2.0?
questions.prompt: For L(x) = x², gradient = 2x. Starting at x = 3, with learning rate 2.0, what is x after one step?
questions.feedback: x ← 3 - 2.0 * (2*3) = 3 - 12 = -9. The step overshoots past zero!

questions.title: GD step for linear regression
questions.prompt: Implement gradient_descent_step(w, b, X, y, lr) that takes ONE GD step on MSE loss. Gradients: dL/dw = (2/n)*sum((w*x+b-y)*x); dL/db = (2/n)*sum(w*x+b-y). Return (new_w, new_b).
questions.code:
def gradient_descent_step(w, b, X, y, lr):
    n = len(X)
    # Compute predictions, gradients, update
    pass
questions.feedback: preds=[w*x+b for x in X]; dw=2/n*sum((p-t)*x for p,t,x in zip(preds,y,X)); db=2/n*sum(p-t for p,t in zip(preds,y))

questions.title: Batch variants
questions.prompt: Match each gradient descent variant to its description.

matchingPairs.right: Uses all N samples per step — stable but slow
Uses 1 sample per step — noisy but fast
Uses k samples per step — the practical default

questions.feedback: Mini-batch GD (k=32 to 256) is used in virtually every modern deep learning framework.

questions.title: Why SGD noise can help
questions.prompt: Stochastic GD (1 sample/step) produces noisy updates. Why can this be beneficial?

choices.text: Noise makes training faster on a single CPU
Noise can help escape sharp local minima
Noise improves the loss function itself

questions.feedback: Random noise acts like small kicks that can bounce the model out of sharp, overfit minima.

questions.title: Order the training loop
questions.prompt: Arrange the steps of one gradient descent epoch in the correct order.
questions.rawMarkdown:
Blocks:
```python
update: w = w - lr * gradient
compute gradient of loss w.r.t. w
compute loss L(w)
repeat until convergence
forward pass: predictions = model(X)
```
*Correct order:*
```python
forward pass: predictions = model(X)
compute loss L(w)
compute gradient of loss w.r.t. w
update: w = w - lr * gradient
repeat until convergence
```
questions.feedback: Forward → Loss → Gradient → Update → Repeat. This is the training loop.

questions.title: Lab: Decision Boundary
questions.prompt: Slide the threshold to separate blue dots (class 0) from red dots (class 1). Yellow border = misclassified. Find the threshold that maximises accuracy.
questions.feedback: A decision boundary separates feature space into class regions. Even a single threshold can handle linearly separable data.

questions.title: What form does a logistic boundary take?
questions.prompt: In 2D feature space, what form does a logistic regression decision boundary take?
questions.feedback: Logistic regression is a linear classifier — its boundary is a line in 2D.

questions.title: Why sigmoid?
questions.prompt:
For classification we want outputs in (0,1) — probabilities. We apply sigmoid to the linear output z = w*x + b:

  sigma(z) = 1 / (1 + e^(-z))

Outputs > 0.5 → class 1; below 0.5 → class 0. The 0.5 cut is the decision boundary.
questions.feedback: Sigmoid squashes any real number into (0,1) — perfect for probabilities.

questions.title: Compute sigmoid
questions.prompt: Implement sigmoid(z) = 1 / (1 + exp(-z)).
questions.feedback: return 1 / (1 + math.exp(-z))

questions.title: Lab: Confusion Matrix
questions.prompt: Adjust the classification threshold and watch TP, FP, TN, FN, precision, and recall change in real time. Notice the precision-recall tradeoff.
questions.feedback: High recall = rarely misses positives. High precision = rarely raises false alarms. You cannot maximise both at once.

questions.title: TP, FP, TN, FN
questions.prompt: Match each term to its meaning.

matchingPairs.right: Predicted positive, actually positive
Predicted positive, actually negative
Predicted negative, actually negative
Predicted negative, actually positive

questions.feedback: True = model was right. Positive/Negative = what the model predicted.

questions.title: Precision vs recall
questions.prompt: A cancer screening model should almost never miss a positive case, even with some false alarms. Which metric should be maximised?
questions.feedback: Recall measures how many actual positives we caught. Missing a cancer case (FN) is far worse than a false alarm.

questions.title: Compute F1
questions.prompt: Implement f1_score(precision, recall) = 2*P*R / (P+R). Return 0.0 if denominator is 0.
questions.feedback: denom = precision + recall; return 2*precision*recall/denom if denom > 0 else 0.0

questions.title: Which error matters most?
questions.prompt: A spam filter trashes flagged emails. Missing a legitimate email is very bad. Which error type costs the most here?

choices.text: False Negative (legitimate email classified as spam)

questions.feedback: A FN here means a real email was trashed — the costliest error for the user.

questions.title: Lab: Neural Network
questions.prompt: Adjust the input sliders and watch activations flow through the hidden layer to the output. This is the forward pass — data flowing from inputs to predictions.
questions.feedback: Each neuron computes a weighted sum of inputs, then applies an activation function. That is all a neural network layer does.

questions.title: What is the forward pass?
questions.prompt: In the forward pass of a neural network, what happens?

choices.text: Gradients are computed and parameters updated
Input data flows through the network to produce a prediction

questions.feedback: Forward pass = input → layers → output (prediction). Backward pass = gradients and updates.

questions.title: Lab: Activation Functions
questions.prompt: Compare sigmoid, ReLU, and tanh. Drag the input slider to see how each function transforms the same input differently.
questions.feedback: Without activation functions, stacking layers would still produce a linear model. Non-linear activations are what give neural networks their power.

questions.title: Why are activations non-linear?
questions.prompt: Why do neural networks need non-linear activation functions?

choices.text: Without them, many layers collapse into a single linear transformation
They reduce the number of parameters

questions.feedback: A stack of linear layers is just one linear layer. Non-linearity is what lets networks approximate complex functions.

questions.title: Build a forward pass
questions.prompt: Arrange the operations of a single neural network layer in the correct order.
questions.rawMarkdown:
Blocks:
```python
output = activation(z)
z = dot(weights, inputs) + bias
return output
inputs = layer_input
```
*Correct order:*
```python
inputs = layer_input
z = dot(weights, inputs) + bias
output = activation(z)
return output
```
questions.feedback: Linear combination first, then activation — always in this order.

questions.title: One layer forward pass
questions.prompt: Implement layer_forward(inputs, weights, bias, activation) where activation is 'relu' or 'sigmoid'. Return the output vector.
questions.code:
import math

def layer_forward(inputs, weights, bias, activation):
    # weights: list of [w1,w2,...] per neuron
    # inputs: list of input values
    # Return list of neuron outputs
    pass
questions.feedback: z = sum(w*x for w,x in zip(neuron_w, inputs)) + b; sigmoid or relu; return list

questions.title: Backprop in plain English
questions.prompt:
After a forward pass, we compute the loss. Backpropagation uses the chain rule to compute how much each weight contributed to the error — propagating gradients backwards from the output to the input.

The output layer's gradient flows back through hidden layers, getting multiplied by the local derivative at each step. This tells every weight how to adjust to reduce the loss.
questions.feedback: Backprop = chain rule applied layer by layer, from output to input.

questions.title: Vanishing gradients
questions.prompt: When using sigmoid activations in very deep networks, gradients can become extremely small as they propagate backwards. This is called:
questions.feedback: Sigmoid squashes outputs to (0,1) — its derivative is at most 0.25, so multiplying many tiny derivatives causes gradients to vanish.

questions.title: Neural network vocabulary
questions.prompt: Match each term to its definition.

matchingPairs.right: Input flows through layers to produce a prediction
Gradients flow backwards to update weights
Activation: max(0, x) — popular in hidden layers
One complete pass through the training dataset

questions.feedback: These four concepts are the engine of deep learning.

questions.title: Lab: Overfitting
questions.prompt: Move the degree slider from 1 to 9. Blue dots are training points; orange are test points. Watch how train MSE and test MSE diverge at high degrees.
questions.feedback: When a model is too complex, it memorises the training data and fails on new data. The gap between train and test error is the overfitting signal.

questions.title: Degree 9 model behaviour
questions.prompt: When you set degree = 9, the model fits training points almost perfectly but test MSE is high. What is happening?

choices.text: The model is memorising noise in training data
The model needs more training data
The polynomial degree is not high enough

questions.feedback: Very high degree polynomials memorise every training point, including noise. They fail to generalise.

questions.title: Lab: Bias-Variance Tradeoff
questions.prompt: Move the complexity slider. Observe how bias² (blue), variance (red), and total error (yellow) change. Find the sweet spot that minimises total error.
questions.feedback: Every model faces this tradeoff. Too simple = high bias. Too complex = high variance. The minimum of total error is the optimal complexity.

questions.title: Bias-variance relationship
questions.prompt: A model with very high training accuracy but poor test accuracy has high ___ and low ___.
questions.feedback: High variance means the model is too sensitive to training data — it overfits.

questions.title: What is regularisation?
questions.prompt:
Regularisation adds a penalty term to the loss function to discourage large weights:

L2 (Ridge): loss + lambda * sum(w²)  — shrinks all weights toward 0
L1 (Lasso): loss + lambda * sum(|w|) — drives some weights to exactly 0 (sparse)

The lambda hyperparameter controls how much you penalise complexity. Higher lambda = simpler model.
questions.feedback: Regularisation is a tax on complexity — it forces the model to stay simple.

questions.title: L1 vs L2
questions.prompt: You have 100 features but suspect only 5 are truly relevant. Which regulariser automatically removes irrelevant features?
questions.feedback: L1 pushes weights to exactly zero — it performs automatic feature selection.

questions.title: Why cross-validate?
questions.prompt: You tune your model using a fixed validation set and achieve great validation accuracy. But on the final test set, accuracy drops. What went wrong?

choices.text: The validation set was too large
You overfit to the validation set by tuning on it repeatedly
The test set is harder than the validation set

questions.feedback: Repeatedly evaluating on the same validation set leaks information. k-fold CV gives a more honest estimate.

questions.title: k-fold split sizes
questions.prompt: Implement kfold_sizes(n, k) that returns a list of k fold sizes that sum to n. The first (n % k) folds have size floor(n/k)+1; the rest have size floor(n/k).
questions.feedback: base = n // k; extra = n % k; [base+1]*extra + [base]*(k-extra)

questions.title: What is dropout?
questions.prompt:
Dropout randomly turns off neurons during training (sets their output to 0) with probability p. This forces the network to not rely on any single neuron — each subset of neurons must work together.

At test time, all neurons are active but outputs are scaled by (1-p) to account for the expected fraction turned off during training.
questions.feedback: Dropout creates an ensemble of many sub-networks, reducing overfitting.

questions.title: Dropout at test time
questions.prompt: A network trained with dropout p=0.5 on a layer with 100 neurons. At test time, how many neurons are active in that layer?
questions.feedback: At inference time, all neurons activate. Outputs are scaled by (1 - dropout_rate) to match training expectations.

questions.title: Overfitting vocabulary
questions.prompt: Match each term to its description.

matchingPairs.right: High train accuracy, low test accuracy
High bias — model too simple for the data
Drives weights to zero — feature selection
Honest evaluation using k data splits

questions.feedback: These techniques are the practitioner's toolkit for generalisation.

questions.title: Decision tree intuition
questions.prompt:
A decision tree asks a series of yes/no questions about features to classify data. Each internal node is a split condition; leaves are predictions.

Example: Is age > 30? → Is income > 50k? → Predict: will buy = True

Trees are easy to interpret but prone to overfitting (they can memorise the training set by growing very deep).
questions.feedback: Decision trees split the feature space into regions, one question at a time.

questions.title: Tree depth and overfitting
questions.prompt: A decision tree with unlimited depth achieves 100% training accuracy. What is almost certainly happening?

choices.text: The tree has found a perfect classification rule
The tree has memorised the training set — it will overfit

questions.feedback: An unpruned tree can create a leaf for every training point. That is perfect memorisation, not learning.

questions.title: Entropy and information gain
questions.prompt:
To choose which feature to split on, decision trees measure Information Gain:

IG = Entropy(parent) - weighted average Entropy(children)

Entropy H = -sum(p * log2(p)) measures impurity. A pure node (all same class) has H = 0; 50/50 split has H = 1.

We choose the split that maximises IG — the split that most reduces uncertainty.
questions.feedback: Information gain finds the split that creates the purest child nodes.

questions.title: Entropy at 50/50
questions.prompt: A node has 50% class A and 50% class B. What is its entropy H? (Round to 1 decimal)

questions.title: Why random forests work
questions.prompt:
A random forest trains many decision trees, each on a random subset of the data (bagging) and a random subset of features. Predictions are made by majority vote.

Each tree overfits differently, so averaging their predictions cancels out individual errors — reducing variance without increasing bias much.
questions.feedback: Ensemble = many weak learners voting together to make a strong learner.

questions.title: Feature subsets in random forests
questions.prompt: Why does a random forest use a random subset of features at each split?

choices.text: To decorrelate the trees, ensuring they learn different patterns
To prevent any single feature from being used

questions.feedback: If all trees used the same features, they would be correlated and averaging them would not reduce variance.

questions.title: Bagging vs Boosting
questions.prompt: Match each ensemble technique to its description.

matchingPairs.right: Trains independent models in parallel; averages predictions
Trains models sequentially; each corrects previous errors
Bagging with random feature subsets on decision trees
Boosting where each tree fits the residual errors

questions.feedback: Bagging reduces variance; boosting reduces bias. Both improve on single models.

questions.title: XGBoost type
questions.prompt: XGBoost is one of the most popular ML algorithms for tabular data. What type of ensemble is it?
questions.feedback: XGBoost stands for Extreme Gradient Boosting — it is a highly optimised gradient boosting framework.

questions.title: Best threshold finder
questions.prompt: Implement best_split_threshold(values, labels) that finds the threshold minimising total weighted impurity (Gini). Gini impurity = 1 - sum(p_i^2). Try all midpoints between sorted unique values. Return (threshold, gini_score) as a tuple.
questions.code:
def best_split_threshold(values, labels):
    sorted_vals = sorted(set(values))
    best = None
    best_gini = float('inf')
    for i in range(len(sorted_vals) - 1):
        thresh = (sorted_vals[i] + sorted_vals[i+1]) / 2
        left = [l for v, l in zip(values, labels) if v <= thresh]
        right = [l for v, l in zip(values, labels) if v > thresh]
        # Compute weighted Gini
        pass
    return (best, best_gini)
questions.feedback: For each side: gini = 1 - sum((labels.count(c)/len(labels))**2 for c in set(labels)); weighted = (len(left)*gl + len(right)*gr) / len(labels)

questions.title: Tree vocabulary
questions.prompt: Match each term to its description.

matchingPairs.right: Measure of impurity in a node
Reduction in entropy from a split
Limiting tree depth to prevent overfitting
Error on samples not used in a bagged tree

questions.feedback: Decision trees and ensembles dominate tabular ML competitions for good reason.

questions.title: Lab: K-Means Clustering
questions.prompt: Press Step to run one K-means iteration. Watch centroids (C1, C2, C3) move toward cluster centres and data points change colour as they are reassigned.
questions.feedback: K-means alternates between: (1) assign each point to the nearest centroid, (2) move each centroid to the mean of its assigned points. Repeat until convergence.

questions.title: K-means convergence
questions.prompt: K-means converges when the centroids stop ___.
questions.feedback: K-means has converged when no centroid changes position between iterations.

questions.title: How to choose K
questions.prompt:
K-means requires you to specify K in advance. A common approach is the elbow method:

1. Run K-means for K = 1, 2, ..., 10
2. Plot the within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS) vs K
3. Look for an 'elbow' — the point where adding more clusters stops reducing WCSS significantly

There is no perfect answer; the elbow is a heuristic.
questions.feedback: The elbow method balances cluster quality against model complexity (number of clusters).

questions.title: WCSS and K
questions.prompt: As K increases from 1 to N (number of data points), WCSS:

choices.text: Always decreases, reaching 0 at K = N

questions.feedback: At K = N, each point is its own cluster, so WCSS = 0. More clusters always fit the data better — the elbow tells us when returns diminish.

questions.title: What is PCA?
questions.prompt:
Principal Component Analysis (PCA) finds the directions (principal components) in which data varies the most.

The first PC captures the most variance; the second PC (orthogonal to the first) captures the next most; and so on.

By projecting data onto the top k PCs, we reduce dimensionality while keeping as much information as possible.
questions.feedback: PCA rotates data to align with directions of maximum variance, then drops the least important ones.

questions.title: PCA use case
questions.prompt: You have 500 features but want to visualise your data in 2D. What is the right tool?

choices.text: PCA — project onto the top 2 principal components

questions.feedback: PCA is the standard technique for reducing high-dimensional data to 2D or 3D for visualisation.

questions.title: What is anomaly detection?
questions.prompt:
Anomaly detection (also called outlier detection) identifies data points that do not fit the expected pattern — without needing labelled anomaly examples.

Approaches include:
• Statistical methods (z-score, IQR)
• Isolation Forest (randomly isolates points — anomalies isolate faster)
• Autoencoder reconstruction error

Applications: fraud detection, network intrusion, manufacturing defects.
questions.feedback: Anomaly detection finds needles in haystacks — without being told what the needle looks like.

questions.title: Why anomaly detection needs no labels
questions.prompt: Why is unsupervised anomaly detection often preferable to supervised classification?

choices.text: Supervised models are slower to train
Anomalies are rare and varied — labelled examples are scarce
Unsupervised models are always more accurate
Supervised models cannot handle numerical features

questions.feedback: You cannot label what you have not seen yet. Anomalies are rare and novel — unsupervised methods can flag the unknown.

questions.title: Core concepts review
questions.prompt: Match each concept to its description.

matchingPairs.right: Optimisation by following the gradient of the loss downhill
Penalty on model complexity to improve generalisation
Chain rule applied backwards to compute weight gradients
Iterative clustering into K groups by centroid proximity

questions.feedback: Congratulations — you have completed the Machine Learning course!
